content.plain_english: This week in Year 4 maths, your child is working on Fractions. The learning focuses on: solve addition and subtraction two-step problems in contexts, deciding which operations and methods to use and why; estimate, compare and calculate different measures, including money in pounds and pence; and measure and calculate the perimeter of a rectilinear figure (including squares) in centimetres and metres. These skills build directly on what they have already mastered and prepare them for the next steps in the programme of study.
content.in_school: Children begin by folding paper, using fraction bars, and drawing bar models to see the concept visually before working with numbers and symbols. Discussion in pairs and small groups helps them explain and justify their reasoning using precise mathematical vocabulary.
content.home_activity: Take a piece of paper and fold it into equal parts — halves, then quarters, then eighths. Shade some sections and ask: 'What fraction is shaded? What fraction is not?' A pizza or sandwich works just as well. Keep it visual and practical.
dinner_table_questions: Can you show me how to solve addition and subtraction two-step problems in contexts, deciding which operations and methods to use and why?
Was it easy or tricky to estimate, compare and calculate different measures, including money in pounds and pence? Why?
How would you explain today's maths to someone who has never seen it before?

key_vocabulary.definition: The top number in a fraction — shows how many equal parts we have.
The bottom number in a fraction — shows how many equal parts the whole is split into.
Fractions that look different but have the same value — e.g. ½ = 2/4.
A fraction with 1 as the numerator — such as ½, ⅓, ¼.
A number with a whole number part and a fraction part — e.g. 2½.

content.times_table_tip: Year 4 children should know all their times tables to 12 × 12 by the end of the year — the national Multiplication Tables Check happens in Year 4. Daily 3-minute practice mixing tables in random order is the best preparation.
